Assignment 3 - Part 1 - Voice In Schizophrenia

Riccardo Fusaroli

August 09, 2019

Assignment 3 - Part 1 - Assessing voice in schizophrenia

Individuals with schizophrenia (SCZ) tend to present voice atypicalities. Their tone is described as “inappropriate” voice, sometimes monotone, sometimes croaky. This is important for two reasons. First, voice could constitute a direct window into cognitive, emotional and social components of the disorder, thus providing a cheap and relatively non-invasive way to support the diagnostic and assessment process (via automated analyses). Second, voice atypicalities play an important role in the social impairment experienced by individuals with SCZ, and are thought to generate negative social judgments (of unengaged, slow, unpleasant interlocutors), which can cascade in more negative and less frequent social interactions.

Several studies show significant differences in acoustic features by diagnosis (see meta-analysis in the readings), but we want more. We want to know whether we can diagnose a participant only from knowing the features of their voice.

The corpus you are asked to analyse is a relatively large set of voice recordings from people with schizophrenia (just after first diagnosis) and matched controls (on gender, age, education). Each participant watched several videos of triangles moving across the screen and had to describe them (so you have several recordings per person). We have already extracted the pitch once every 10 milliseconds as well as several duration related features (e.g. number of pauses, etc).

N.B. For the fun of it, I threw in data from 3 different languages: 1) Danish (study 1-4); 2) Mandarin Chinese (Study 5-6); 3) Japanese (study 7). Feel free to only use the Danish data, if you think that Mandarin and Japanese add too much complexity to your analysis.

In this assignment (A3), you will have to discuss a few important questions (given the data you have). More details below.

Part 1 - Can we find a difference in acoustic features in schizophrenia? 1) Describe your sample number of studies, number of participants, age, gender, clinical and cognitive features of the two groups. Furthemore, critically assess whether the groups (schizophrenia and controls) are balanced. N.B. you need to take studies into account.

  1. Describe the acoustic profile of a schizophrenic voice: which features are different? E.g. People with schizophrenia tend to have high-pitched voice, and present bigger swings in their prosody than controls. N.B. look also at effect sizes. How do these findings relate to the meta-analytic findings?

  2. Discuss the analysis necessary to replicate the meta-analytic findings Look at the results reported in the paper (see meta-analysis in the readings) and see whether they are similar to those you get. 3.1) Check whether significance and direction of the effects are similar 3.2) Standardize your outcome, run the model and check whether the beta’s is roughly matched (matched with hedge’s g) which fixed and random effects should be included, given your dataset? E.g. what about language and study, age and gender? Discuss also how studies and languages should play a role in your analyses. E.g. should you analyze each study individually? Or each language individually? Or all together? Each of these choices makes some assumptions about how similar you expect the studies/languages to be. Note that there is no formal definition of replication (in statistical terms).

Your report should look like a methods paragraph followed by a result paragraph in a typical article (think the Communication and Cognition paper)

Part 2 - Can we diagnose schizophrenia from voice only? 1) Discuss whether you should you run the analysis on all studies and both languages at the same time You might want to support your results either by your own findings or by that of others 2) Choose your best acoustic feature from part 1. How well can you diagnose schizophrenia just using it? 3) Identify the best combination of acoustic features to diagnose schizophrenia using logistic regression. 4) Discuss the “classification” process: which methods are you using? Which confounds should you be aware of? What are the strength and limitation of the analysis?

Bonus question: Logistic regression is only one of many classification algorithms. Try using others and compare performance. Some examples: Discriminant Function, Random Forest, Support Vector Machine, Penalized regression, etc. The packages caret and glmnet provide them. Tidymodels is a set of tidyverse style packages, which take some time to learn, but provides a great workflow for machine learning.

Learning objectives

  • Critically design, fit and report multilevel regression models in complex settings
  • Critically appraise issues of replication

Overview of part 1

In the course of this part 1 of Assignment 3 you have to: - combine the different information from multiple files into one meaningful dataset you can use for your analysis. This involves: extracting descriptors of acoustic features from each pitch file (e.g. mean/median, standard deviation / interquartile range), and combine them with duration and demographic/clinical files - describe and discuss your sample - analyze the meaningful dataset to assess whether there are indeed differences in the schizophrenic voice and compare that to the meta-analysis

There are three pieces of data:

1- Demographic data (https://www.dropbox.com/s/e2jy5fyac18zld7/DemographicData.csv?dl=0). It contains

  • Study: a study identifier (the recordings were collected during 6 different studies with 6 different clinical practitioners in 2 different languages)
  • Language: Danish, Chinese and Japanese
  • Participant: a subject ID
  • Diagnosis: whether the participant has schizophrenia or is a control
  • Gender
  • Education
  • Age
  • SANS: total score of negative symptoms (including lack of motivation, affect, etc). Ref: Andreasen, N. C. (1989). The Scale for the Assessment of Negative Symptoms (SANS): conceptual and theoretical foundations. The British Journal of Psychiatry, 155(S7), 49-52.
  • SAPS: total score of positive symptoms (including psychoses, such as delusions and hallucinations): http://www.bli.uzh.ch/BLI/PDF/saps.pdf
  • VerbalIQ: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wechsler_Adult_Intelligence_Scale
  • NonVerbalIQ: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wechsler_Adult_Intelligence_Scale
  • TotalIQ: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wechsler_Adult_Intelligence_Scale
  1. Articulation.txt (https://www.dropbox.com/s/vuyol7b575xdkjm/Articulation.txt?dl=0). It contains, per each file, measures of duration:
  • soundname: the name of the recording file
  • nsyll: number of syllables automatically inferred from the audio
  • npause: number of pauses automatically inferred from the audio (absence of human voice longer than 200 milliseconds)
  • dur (s): duration of the full recording
  • phonationtime (s): duration of the recording where speech is present
  • speechrate (nsyll/dur): average number of syllables per second
  • articulation rate (nsyll / phonationtime): average number of syllables per spoken second
  • ASD (speakingtime/nsyll): average syllable duration
  1. One file per recording with the fundamental frequency of speech extracted every 10 milliseconds (excluding pauses): https://www.dropbox.com/sh/bfnzaf8xgxrv37u/AAD2k6SX4rJBHo7zzRML7cS9a?dl=0
  • time: the time at which fundamental frequency was sampled
  • f0: a measure of fundamental frequency, in Herz

NB. the filenames indicate: - Study: the study, 1-6 (1-4 in Danish, 5-6 in Mandarin Chinese) - D: the diagnosis, 0 is control, 1 is schizophrenia - S: the subject ID (NB. some controls and schizophrenia are matched, so there is a 101 schizophrenic and a 101 control). Also note that study 5-6 have weird numbers and no matched participants, so feel free to add e.g. 1000 to the participant ID in those studies. - T: the trial, that is, the recording ID for that participant, 1-10 (note that study 5-6 have more)

Getting to the pitch data

You have oh so many pitch files. What you want is a neater dataset, with one row per recording, including a bunch of meaningful descriptors of pitch. For instance, we should include “standard” descriptors: mean, standard deviation, range. Additionally, we should also include less standard, but more robust ones: e.g. median, iqr, mean absoluted deviation, coefficient of variation. The latter ones are more robust to outliers and non-normal distributions.

Tip: Load one file (as a sample) and: - write code to extract the descriptors - write code to extract the relevant information from the file names (Participant, Diagnosis, Trial, Study) Only then (when everything works) turn the code into a function and use map_df() to apply it to all the files. See placeholder code here for help.

#ANDERS 

pacman::p_load(tidyverse, EnvStats, DescTools, lme4, effsize)
#SIGRID

#Making a function that reads the pitch
read_pitch <- function(filename) {
    # load data
    path_name <- str_c("Pitch/",filename)
    data <- read_tsv(path_name)
   
    #CLeaning the data and comprimising it
    data <- data %>% 
        mutate(
             # parse filename to extract study, diagnosis, subject and trial
            Study = str_extract(filename, "Study\\d"),
            Study = str_extract(Study, "\\d"),
            Diagnosis = str_extract(filename, "D\\d"),
            Diagnosis = recode(Diagnosis, 
                "D0" = "Control",
                "D1" = "Schizophrenia"),
            Participant =  str_extract(filename, "S\\d{3}"),
            Participant = as.integer(str_extract(Participant, "\\d{3}")),
            Participant = ifelse(Study == "5" | Study == "6", Participant + 1000, Participant),
            Participant = as.character(Participant),
            Trial = str_extract(filename, "T\\d{1,2}"),
            Trial = str_extract(Trial, "\\d{1,2}"),
            
            # extract pitch descriptors (mean, sd, iqr, etc)
            mean = mean(f0),
            standard_deviation = sd(f0),
            range_low = range(f0)[1],
            range_high = range(f0)[2],
            median = median(f0),
            IQR = IQR(f0),
            mean_abs = MeanAD(f0),
            coef_var = cv(f0),
            New_id = as.factor(paste0(Participant, Diagnosis))
            ) %>% 
        select(Study:New_id) %>% 
        slice(1) 
    
    # combine all this data in one dataset
    return(data)
}
#GUSTAV 

# test it on just one file while writing the function
test_data = read_pitch("Study6D1S044T22.txt")

# when you've created a function that works, you can
pitch_data = list.files(path = "Pitch/",pattern = ".txt") %>% ## NB replace with your path to the files
    purrr::map_df(read_pitch)

#Filtering incomplete cases out. 
pitch_data <- pitch_data %>% 
    filter(complete.cases(pitch_data)) 

#Exporting the data to a new csv
write_csv(pitch_data, "pitch_data.csv")

Now you need to merge demographic/clinical, duration and pitch data

#ANDERS 

#Reading pitch data
pitch_data <- read_csv("pitch_data.csv") 

# Loading the duration data
dur <- read.csv("Articulation.txt", sep = ",")

#Cleaning the duration data
dur <- dur %>% 
    mutate(
            Study = str_extract(soundname, "Study\\d"),
            Study = str_extract(Study, "\\d"),
            Diagnosis = str_extract(soundname, "D\\d"),
            Diagnosis = recode(Diagnosis, 
                "D0" = "Control",
                "D1" = "Schizophrenia"),
            Participant =  str_extract(soundname, "S\\d{3}"),
            Participant = as.integer(str_extract(Participant, "\\d{3}")),
            Participant = ifelse(Study == "5" | Study == "6", Participant + 1000, Participant),
            Participant = as.character(Participant),
            Trial = str_extract(soundname, "T\\d{1,2}"),
            Trial = str_extract(Trial, "\\d{1,2}"),
            New_id = as.factor(paste0(Participant, Diagnosis))
    ) %>% 
    filter(
        complete.cases(dur)
        )

#Merging pitch data and duration data
df <- merge(pitch_data, dur, all = T)

# Loading the demographic data
demo <- read.csv("DemographicData.csv", sep = ";") %>% 
    mutate(
        Participant = as.integer(Participant),
        Participant = ifelse(Study == 5 | Study == 6, Participant + 1000, Participant),
        Participant = as.character(Participant),
        Diagnosis = as.character(Diagnosis),
        New_id = as.factor(paste0(Participant, Diagnosis))
    )

#Merging demo with the rest of the data
df1 <- merge(demo, df, all = T) %>%
    filter(Study != 7) %>% 
    rename(
        dur_s = dur..s.,
        phonationtime_s = phonationtime..s.,
        speechrate_nsyll_dur = speechrate..nsyll.dur.,
        articulation_rate_nsyll_phonationtime = articulation.rate..nsyll...phonationtime.,
        ASD_speakingtime_nsyll = ASD..speakingtime.nsyll.
    ) %>% 
    mutate(
        Study = as.factor(Study),
        Diagnosis = as.factor(Diagnosis),
    )

#Filtering out NAs
df1 <- df1 %>% 
    filter(
        is.na(Diagnosis) == F,
        is.na(df$dur_s) ==F,
        is.na(df$phonationtime_s) == F, 
        is.na(df$npause) == F
        )

# Now we save them
write_csv(df1, "FINAL_DATA.csv")

Now we need to describe our sample

#SIGRID 

#Loading the final dataset
df <- read.csv("FINAL_DATA.csv") %>% 
    mutate(Study = as.factor(Study))

Now we need to describe our sample

First look at the missing data: we should exclude all recordings for which we do not have complete data. Then count the participants and recordinsgs by diagnosis, report their gender, age and symptom severity (SANS, SAPS and Social) Finally, do the same by diagnosis and study, to assess systematic differences in studies. I like to use group_by() %>% summarize() for quick summaries

#GUSTAV 

#Summarizing by diagnosis
df %>% 
    split(df$Diagnosis) %>% 
    map(summary)

#ANDERS 

#Summarizing by gender
df %>% 
    split(df$Gender) %>% 
    map(summary)
## $F
##  Study           Diagnosis    Participant                   New_id    
##  1:318   Control      :738   Min.   : 102.0   1022Schizophrenia:  42  
##  2:103   Schizophrenia:714   1st Qu.: 209.0   1026Schizophrenia:  41  
##  3:188                       Median : 423.0   1204Control      :  32  
##  4:202                       Mean   : 599.2   1202Control      :  29  
##  5: 61                       3rd Qu.:1022.0   1033Control      :  28  
##  6:580                       Max.   :1204.0   1001Schizophrenia:  23  
##                                               (Other)          :1257  
##     Language   Gender        Age         Education          SANS       
##  Chinese:641   F:1452   Min.   :18.0   Min.   : 9.00   Min.   : 0.000  
##  Danish :811   M:   0   1st Qu.:21.0   1st Qu.:11.00   1st Qu.: 0.000  
##                         Median :25.0   Median :13.50   Median : 5.000  
##                         Mean   :27.6   Mean   :13.57   Mean   : 5.045  
##                         3rd Qu.:30.0   3rd Qu.:16.00   3rd Qu.: 8.000  
##                         Max.   :59.0   Max.   :21.00   Max.   :16.000  
##                         NA's   :8                      NA's   :503     
##       SAPS           VerbalIQ      NonVerbalIQ        TotalIQ      
##  Min.   : 0.000   Min.   : 60.0   Min.   : 39.00   Min.   : 48.00  
##  1st Qu.: 0.000   1st Qu.: 83.0   1st Qu.: 79.00   1st Qu.: 84.00  
##  Median : 7.000   Median : 95.0   Median : 94.00   Median : 95.00  
##  Mean   : 6.276   Mean   : 95.9   Mean   : 92.26   Mean   : 94.63  
##  3rd Qu.:11.000   3rd Qu.:106.0   3rd Qu.:111.00   3rd Qu.:106.00  
##  Max.   :18.000   Max.   :132.0   Max.   :133.00   Max.   :132.00  
##  NA's   :503      NA's   :451     NA's   :451      NA's   :451     
##      Trial             mean        standard_deviation   range_low     
##  Min.   : 1.000   Min.   : 67.88   Min.   :  4.823    Min.   : 45.65  
##  1st Qu.: 3.000   1st Qu.:186.36   1st Qu.: 17.980    1st Qu.:133.20

#Summarizing by study
df %>% 
    split(df$Study) %>% 
    map(summary)

##  Study            Diagnosis    Participant                 New_id   
##  1:   0   Control      :599   Min.   :1001   1037Schizophrenia: 58

##  4:   0                       Mean   :1023   1008Schizophrenia: 31  
##  5:   0                       3rd Qu.:1035   1033Control      : 28  
##  6:1225                       Max.   :1044   1006Schizophrenia: 27

##     Language    Gender       Age          Education          SANS

##  Danish :   0   M:645   1st Qu.:23.00   1st Qu.:11.00   1st Qu.: 3.000  
##                         Median :27.00   Median :13.00   Median : 5.000  
##                         Mean   :29.87   Mean   :13.01   Mean   : 5.348  
##                         3rd Qu.:35.00   3rd Qu.:15.00   3rd Qu.: 8.000  
##                         Max.   :52.00   Max.   :17.00   Max.   :12.000

##       SAPS           VerbalIQ       NonVerbalIQ        TotalIQ      
##  Min.   : 0.000   Min.   : 60.00   Min.   : 39.00   Min.   : 48.00  
##  1st Qu.: 7.000   1st Qu.: 86.00   1st Qu.: 80.00   1st Qu.: 88.00  
##  Median :10.000   Median : 95.00   Median : 95.00   Median : 98.00  
##  Mean   : 9.497   Mean   : 96.35   Mean   : 99.26   Mean   : 97.99  
##  3rd Qu.:12.000   3rd Qu.:106.00   3rd Qu.:119.00   3rd Qu.:111.00  
##  Max.   :15.000   Max.   :133.00   Max.   :143.00   Max.   :132.00  
##  NA's   :599                                                        
##      Trial            mean        standard_deviation   range_low     
##  Min.   : 1.00   Min.   : 60.76   Min.   :  1.471    Min.   : 43.49  
##  1st Qu.: 5.00   1st Qu.:122.61   1st Qu.: 13.646    1st Qu.: 88.87  
##  Median :10.00   Median :167.42   Median : 20.298    Median :119.50  
##  Mean   :11.57   Mean   :171.59   Mean   : 25.488    Mean   :126.71  
##  3rd Qu.:16.00   3rd Qu.:218.35   3rd Qu.: 29.908    3rd Qu.:162.87  
##  Max.   :58.00   Max.   :431.01   Max.   :281.311    Max.   :250.53  
##                                                                      
##    range_high         median            IQR              mean_abs       
##  Min.   : 64.49   Min.   : 57.68   Min.   :  0.6025   Min.   :  0.9038  
##  1st Qu.:191.63   1st Qu.:115.93   1st Qu.: 15.3000   1st Qu.: 10.4706  
##  Median :239.19   Median :164.38   Median : 23.5300   Median : 15.3859  
##  Mean   :249.09   Mean   :167.17   Mean   : 34.1256   Mean   : 20.4055  
##  3rd Qu.:293.24   3rd Qu.:214.43   3rd Qu.: 38.1100   3rd Qu.: 23.1450  
##  Max.   :912.80   Max.   :465.50   Max.   :537.6400   Max.   :273.9067

##     coef_var                 soundname        nsyll       
##  Min.   :0.01328   Study6D0S001T1 :   1   Min.   :  0.00  
##  1st Qu.:0.08487   Study6D0S001T10:   1   1st Qu.:  6.00  
##  Median :0.12034   Study6D0S001T11:   1   Median : 14.00  
##  Mean   :0.15152   Study6D0S001T12:   1   Mean   : 25.07  
##  3rd Qu.:0.17282   Study6D0S001T13:   1   3rd Qu.: 30.00  
##  Max.   :0.85766   Study6D0S001T2 :   1   Max.   :258.00  
##                    (Other)        :1219                   
##      npause           dur_s        phonationtime_s  speechrate_nsyll_dur
##  Min.   : 0.000   Min.   : 0.500   Min.   : 0.190   Min.   :0.000       
##  1st Qu.: 0.000   1st Qu.: 1.900   1st Qu.: 1.300   1st Qu.:2.500       
##  Median : 1.000   Median : 3.900   Median : 2.750   Median :3.330       
##  Mean   : 2.731   Mean   : 7.347   Mean   : 5.034   Mean   :3.302       
##  3rd Qu.: 4.000   3rd Qu.: 9.000   3rd Qu.: 5.970   3rd Qu.:4.170       
##  Max.   :51.000   Max.   :95.400   Max.   :63.600   Max.   :7.500       
##                                                                         
##  articulation_rate_nsyll_phonationtime ASD_speakingtime_nsyll
##  Min.   :0.000                          0.197 :  25          
##  1st Qu.:4.020                          0.183 :  23          
##  Median :4.860                          0.196 :  21          
##  Mean   :4.644                          0.203 :  21          
##  3rd Qu.:5.430                          0.189 :  19          
##  Max.   :8.510                          0.198 :  18          
##                                        (Other):1098

Now we can analyze the data

#GUSTAV 

#Defining the function for converting estimates into probabilities. 
logit2prob <- function(logit){
    odds = exp(logit)
    prob = odds/(1+odds)
    return(prob)
}
#ANDERS 
#Making the new variables to investigate
df <- df %>%
    mutate(
        pitch_variability = range_high-range_low,
        scaled_pitch_variability = scale(pitch_variability),
        proportion_spoken = phonationtime_s/dur_s,
        scaled_proportion_spoken = scale(proportion_spoken),
        scaled_speech_rate = scale(speechrate_nsyll_dur),
        npause_1 = ifelse(npause == 0, 1, npause),
        pause_duration = (dur_s - phonationtime_s)/npause_1,
        scaled_pause_duration = scale(pause_duration)
        )

If you were to examine the meta analysis you would find that the differences (measured as Hedges’ g, very close to Cohen’s d, that is, in standard deviations) to be the following - pitch variability (lower, Hedges’ g: -0.55, 95% CIs: -1.06, 0.09)

################################### PITCH VARIABILITY#################################
# SIGRID 

#Exploring difference between language according to pitch variability
l1 <- lmerTest::lmer(scaled_pitch_variability ~ Language + (1|New_id) + (1|Study), data = df)
summary(l1)
## Linear mixed model fit by REML. t-tests use Satterthwaite's method [
## lmerModLmerTest]
## Formula: scaled_pitch_variability ~ Language + (1 | New_id) + (1 | Study)
##    Data: df
## 
## REML criterion at convergence: 7735.7
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -5.2923 -0.4397 -0.0795  0.2990  8.1297 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups   Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  New_id   (Intercept) 0.58571  0.7653  
##  Study    (Intercept) 0.04677  0.2163  
##  Residual             0.47518  0.6893  
## Number of obs: 3322, groups:  New_id, 295; Study, 6
## 
## Fixed effects:
##                Estimate Std. Error      df t value Pr(>|t|)
## (Intercept)     -0.2307     0.2012  4.9234  -1.147    0.304
## LanguageDanish   0.4365     0.2348  4.5569   1.859    0.128
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##             (Intr)
## LanguagDnsh -0.857
#Language does not significantly predict pitch variability why we will continue our analysis with both chinese and danish participants in one pool.
#making model -  Significant
m1 <- glmer(Diagnosis ~ scaled_pitch_variability + (1|New_id) + (1|Study), df, family = 'binomial')
## boundary (singular) fit: see ?isSingular
summary(m1)
## Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace
##   Approximation) [glmerMod]
##  Family: binomial  ( logit )
## Formula: Diagnosis ~ scaled_pitch_variability + (1 | New_id) + (1 | Study)
##    Data: df
## 
##      AIC      BIC   logLik deviance df.resid 
##    453.7    478.2   -222.9    445.7     3326 
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##       Min        1Q    Median        3Q       Max 
## -0.000970 -0.000908 -0.000843  0.019860  0.031854 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  New_id (Intercept) 5347     73.12   
##  Study  (Intercept)    0      0.00   
## Number of obs: 3330, groups:  New_id, 296; Study, 6
## 
## Fixed effects:
##                            Estimate Std. Error  z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)              -1.397e+01  6.328e-04 -22074.9   <2e-16 ***
## scaled_pitch_variability -1.111e-01  6.327e-04   -175.6   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##             (Intr)
## scld_ptch_v 0.000 
## convergence code: 0
## boundary (singular) fit: see ?isSingular
#Scaled pitch variability does significantly predict diagnosis.
#plotting the data
df %>% 
    mutate(
        Diagnosis = recode(Diagnosis, 
           "Control" = 0,
           "Schizophrenia" = 1)) %>% 
    ggplot(aes(scaled_pitch_variability, Diagnosis, color = Diagnosis)) + 
        geom_point() + 
        stat_smooth(method="glm",
        method.args=list(family="binomial"), se = FALSE)+
        labs(title = "Diagnosis by scaled mean pitch")+
        theme_classic()

# getting the probability of participant being schizophrenic at a scaled pitch variability of 0.
m1_prob <- logit2prob(fixef(m1)[1])
m1_prob
##  (Intercept) 
## 8.581503e-07
# At a scaled pitch variability of 0, there is a 99,9 % probability of the participant being schizophrenic 


# investigating Hedge's G
effsize::cohen.d(df$scaled_pitch_variability, df$Diagnosis, hedges.correction = T)
## 
## Hedges's g
## 
## g estimate: 0.1784636 (negligible)
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##     lower     upper 
## 0.1101627 0.2467646
# We get a hedges' g of (0.18, CI[0.11, 0.25]).
  • proportion of spoken time (lower, Hedges’ g: -1.26, 95% CIs: -2.26, 0.25)
################ PROPORTON OF SPOKEN TIME  ############################################
#GUSTAV 

#Exploring difference between language according to pitch variability
l2 <- lmerTest::lmer(scaled_proportion_spoken ~ Language + (1|New_id) + (1|Study), data = df)
summary(l2)
## Linear mixed model fit by REML. t-tests use Satterthwaite's method [
## lmerModLmerTest]
## Formula: scaled_proportion_spoken ~ Language + (1 | New_id) + (1 | Study)
##    Data: df
## 
## REML criterion at convergence: 7900.5
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -4.7506 -0.5276  0.0269  0.5508  3.7860 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups   Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  New_id   (Intercept) 0.3349   0.5787  
##  Study    (Intercept) 0.1030   0.3209  
##  Residual             0.5254   0.7248  
## Number of obs: 3322, groups:  New_id, 295; Study, 6
## 
## Fixed effects:
##                Estimate Std. Error      df t value Pr(>|t|)  
## (Intercept)      0.5529     0.2548  4.2520   2.170   0.0917 .
## LanguageDanish  -0.7993     0.3041  3.9357  -2.628   0.0593 .
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##             (Intr)
## LanguagDnsh -0.838
#Language does not significantly predict proportion spoken why we will continue our analysis with both chinese and danish participants in one pool.
#making model -  not significant
m2 <- glmer(Diagnosis ~ scaled_proportion_spoken + (1|New_id) + (1|Study), df, family = 'binomial')
## boundary (singular) fit: see ?isSingular
summary(m2)
## Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace
##   Approximation) [glmerMod]
##  Family: binomial  ( logit )
## Formula: Diagnosis ~ scaled_proportion_spoken + (1 | New_id) + (1 | Study)
##    Data: df
## 
##      AIC      BIC   logLik deviance df.resid 
##    453.7    478.1   -222.8    445.7     3326 
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##       Min        1Q    Median        3Q       Max 
## -0.001218 -0.000941 -0.000793  0.020271  0.032918 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  New_id (Intercept) 4927     70.19   
##  Study  (Intercept)    0      0.00   
## Number of obs: 3330, groups:  New_id, 296; Study, 6
## 
## Fixed effects:
##                          Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)              -13.8982     0.8537 -16.280   <2e-16 ***
## scaled_proportion_spoken  -0.1809     0.4955  -0.365    0.715    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##             (Intr)
## scld_prprt_ 0.067 
## convergence code: 0
## boundary (singular) fit: see ?isSingular
#Scaled proportion spoken does not significantly predict diagnosis.
#plotting the data
df %>% 
    mutate(
        Diagnosis = recode(Diagnosis, 
           "Control" = 0,
           "Schizophrenia" = 1)) %>% 
    ggplot(aes(scaled_proportion_spoken, Diagnosis, color = Diagnosis)) + 
        geom_point() + 
        stat_smooth(method="glm",
        method.args=list(family="binomial"), se = FALSE)+
        labs(title = "Diagnosis by scaled mean pitch")+
        theme_classic()

# getting the probability of participant being schizophrenic at a scaled pitch variability of 0.
m2_prob <- logit2prob(fixef(m2)[1])
m2_prob
##  (Intercept) 
## 9.206472e-07
# At a scaled pitch variability of 0, there is a 99,9 % probability of the participant being schizophrenic 


# investigating Hedge's G
effsize::cohen.d(df$scaled_proportion_spoken, df$Diagnosis, hedges.correction = T)
## 
## Hedges's g
## 
## g estimate: 0.1182268 (negligible)
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##      lower      upper 
## 0.05000139 0.18645224
# We get a hedges' g of (0.12, CI[0.05, 0.19]).
  • speech rate (slower, Hedges’ g: -0.75, 95% CIs: -1.51, 0.04)
################################## SPEECH RATE ##########################################
# ANDERS

#Exploring difference between language according to pitch variability
l3 <- lmerTest::lmer(scaled_speech_rate ~ Language + (1|New_id) + (1|Study), data = df)
summary(l3)
## Linear mixed model fit by REML. t-tests use Satterthwaite's method [
## lmerModLmerTest]
## Formula: scaled_speech_rate ~ Language + (1 | New_id) + (1 | Study)
##    Data: df
## 
## REML criterion at convergence: 8415
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -4.7111 -0.5162  0.0255  0.5456  4.2168 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups   Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  New_id   (Intercept) 0.30600  0.5532  
##  Study    (Intercept) 0.05192  0.2279  
##  Residual             0.62607  0.7912  
## Number of obs: 3322, groups:  New_id, 295; Study, 6
## 
## Fixed effects:
##                Estimate Std. Error      df t value Pr(>|t|)
## (Intercept)      0.3007     0.1927  4.5765   1.561    0.185
## LanguageDanish  -0.4240     0.2278  4.2021  -1.862    0.133
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##             (Intr)
## LanguagDnsh -0.846
#Language does not significantly predict speech rate why we will continue our analysis with both chinese and danish participants in one pool.
#making model -  Significant
m3 <- glmer(Diagnosis ~ scaled_speech_rate + (1|New_id) + (1|Study), df, family = 'binomial')
## boundary (singular) fit: see ?isSingular
summary(m3)
## Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace
##   Approximation) [glmerMod]
##  Family: binomial  ( logit )
## Formula: Diagnosis ~ scaled_speech_rate + (1 | New_id) + (1 | Study)
##    Data: df
## 
##      AIC      BIC   logLik deviance df.resid 
##    453.7    478.1   -222.8    445.7     3326 
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##       Min        1Q    Median        3Q       Max 
## -0.001245 -0.000938 -0.000816  0.020285  0.032628 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  New_id (Intercept) 4916     70.12   
##  Study  (Intercept)    0      0.00   
## Number of obs: 3330, groups:  New_id, 296; Study, 6
## 
## Fixed effects:
##                    Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)        -13.8858     0.8561 -16.221   <2e-16 ***
## scaled_speech_rate  -0.1856     0.4723  -0.393    0.694    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##             (Intr)
## scld_spch_r 0.036 
## convergence code: 0
## boundary (singular) fit: see ?isSingular
#Scaled speech rate does not significantly predict diagnosis.
#plotting the data
df %>% 
    mutate(
        Diagnosis = recode(Diagnosis, 
           "Control" = 0,
           "Schizophrenia" = 1)) %>% 
    ggplot(aes(scaled_speech_rate, Diagnosis, color = Diagnosis)) + 
        geom_point() + 
        stat_smooth(method="glm",
        method.args=list(family="binomial"), se = FALSE)+
        labs(title = "Diagnosis by scaled mean pitch")+
        theme_classic()

# getting the probability of participant being schizophrenic at a scaled speech rate of 0.
m3_prop <- logit2prob(fixef(m3)[1])
m3_prop
##  (Intercept) 
## 9.320998e-07
# At a scaled pitch variability of 0, there is a 99,9 % probability of the participant being schizophrenic 


# investigating Hedge's G
effsize::cohen.d(df$scaled_speech_rate, df$Diagnosis, hedges.correction = T)
## 
## Hedges's g
## 
## g estimate: 0.1725807 (negligible)
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##     lower     upper 
## 0.1042885 0.2408730
# We get a hedges' g of (0.17, CI[0.10, 0.24]).
  • pause duration (longer, Hedges’ g: 1.89, 95% CIs: 0.72, 3.21). (Duration - Spoken Duration) / PauseN
############################### PAUSE DURATION ###################################
# SIGRID

#Exploring difference between language according to pitch variability
l4 <- lmerTest::lmer(scaled_pause_duration ~ Language + (1|New_id) + (1|Study), data = df)
## Warning in checkConv(attr(opt, "derivs"), opt$par, ctrl =
## control$checkConv, : Model failed to converge with max|grad| = 0.0100671
## (tol = 0.002, component 1)
summary(l4)
## Linear mixed model fit by REML. t-tests use Satterthwaite's method [
## lmerModLmerTest]
## Formula: scaled_pause_duration ~ Language + (1 | New_id) + (1 | Study)
##    Data: df
## 
## REML criterion at convergence: 8695.2
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -3.4570 -0.4266 -0.1368  0.2536 12.8287 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups   Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  New_id   (Intercept) 0.27049  0.5201  
##  Study    (Intercept) 0.02852  0.1689  
##  Residual             0.69254  0.8322  
## Number of obs: 3322, groups:  New_id, 295; Study, 6
## 
## Fixed effects:
##                Estimate Std. Error      df t value Pr(>|t|)  
## (Intercept)     -0.3447     0.1524  3.8925  -2.262   0.0883 .
## LanguageDanish   0.5332     0.1789  3.6063   2.981   0.0464 *
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##             (Intr)
## LanguagDnsh -0.852
## convergence code: 0
## Model failed to converge with max|grad| = 0.0100671 (tol = 0.002, component 1)
#Language does significantly predict pitch variability why we will make separate analysis for chinese and danish participants
#DANSKEREN - Non significant:
m4_DK <- glmer(Diagnosis ~ scaled_pause_duration + (1|New_id) + (1|Study), 
               df %>% filter(Language == "Danish"), 
               family = 'binomial')
## boundary (singular) fit: see ?isSingular
summary(m4_DK)
## Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace
##   Approximation) [glmerMod]
##  Family: binomial  ( logit )
## Formula: Diagnosis ~ scaled_pause_duration + (1 | New_id) + (1 | Study)
##    Data: df %>% filter(Language == "Danish")
## 
##      AIC      BIC   logLik deviance df.resid 
##    349.4    371.6   -170.7    341.4     1892 
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##       Min        1Q    Median        3Q       Max 
## -0.001790 -0.001158 -0.001104  0.023179  0.033850 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  New_id (Intercept) 4474     66.89   
##  Study  (Intercept)    0      0.00   
## Number of obs: 1896, groups:  New_id, 221; Study, 4
## 
## Fixed effects:
##                       Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)           -13.4627     0.9749 -13.810   <2e-16 ***
## scaled_pause_duration   0.1776     0.5673   0.313    0.754    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##             (Intr)
## scld_ps_drt -0.092
## convergence code: 0
## boundary (singular) fit: see ?isSingular
#Danskeren pause duration does not significantly predict diagnosis
#NESEREN - significant: 
m4_Chinese <- glmer(Diagnosis ~ scaled_pause_duration + (1|New_id) + (1|Study), 
               df %>% filter(Language == "Chinese"), 
               family = 'binomial')
## Warning in checkConv(attr(opt, "derivs"), opt$par, ctrl =
## control$checkConv, : Model failed to converge with max|grad| = 0.0500848
## (tol = 0.001, component 1)
## Warning in checkConv(attr(opt, "derivs"), opt$par, ctrl = control$checkConv, : Model is nearly unidentifiable: very large eigenvalue
##  - Rescale variables?
summary(m4_Chinese)
## Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace
##   Approximation) [glmerMod]
##  Family: binomial  ( logit )
## Formula: Diagnosis ~ scaled_pause_duration + (1 | New_id) + (1 | Study)
##    Data: df %>% filter(Language == "Chinese")
## 
##      AIC      BIC   logLik deviance df.resid 
##    111.4    132.5    -51.7    103.4     1422 
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##        Min         1Q     Median         3Q        Max 
## -0.0005937 -0.0005650 -0.0003127  0.0118455  0.0199077 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  New_id (Intercept) 7350.95  85.738  
##  Study  (Intercept)   12.49   3.534  
## Number of obs: 1426, groups:  New_id, 74; Study, 2
## 
## Fixed effects:
##                         Estimate Std. Error   z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)           -16.161995   0.001412 -11447.45   <2e-16 ***
## scaled_pause_duration  -0.075161   0.001412    -53.24   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##             (Intr)
## scld_ps_drt 0.000 
## convergence code: 0
## Model failed to converge with max|grad| = 0.0500848 (tol = 0.001, component 1)
## Model is nearly unidentifiable: very large eigenvalue
##  - Rescale variables?
#Neseren pause duration does significantly predict diagnosis
#plotting the data
df %>% 
    filter(Language == "Chinese") %>% 
    mutate(
        Diagnosis = recode(Diagnosis, 
           "Control" = 0,
           "Schizophrenia" = 1)) %>% 
    ggplot(aes(scaled_pitch_variability, Diagnosis, color = Diagnosis)) + 
        geom_point() + 
        stat_smooth(method="glm",
        method.args=list(family="binomial"), se = FALSE)+
        labs(title = "Diagnosis by scaled mean pitch")+
        theme_classic()

# getting the probability of participant being schizophrenic at a scaled pitch variability of 0.
m4_prob <- logit2prob(fixef(m4_Chinese)[1])
m4_prob
##  (Intercept) 
## 9.570503e-08
# At a scaled pitch variability of 0, there is a 99,9 % probability of the participant being schizophrenic 


# investigating Hedge's G
df_chinese <- df %>% 
    filter(Language == "Chinese")  

effsize::cohen.d(df_chinese$scaled_pause_duration, df_chinese$Diagnosis, hedges.correction = T)
## 
## Hedges's g
## 
## g estimate: -0.003933278 (negligible)
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##      lower      upper 
## -0.1085537  0.1006872
# We get a hedges' g of (-0.004, CI[-0.11, 0.10]).

We need therefore to set up 4 models to see how well our results compare to the meta-analytic findings (Feel free of course to test more features) Describe the acoustic profile of a schizophrenic voice Note in this section you need to describe the acoustic profile of a schizophrenic voice and compare it with the meta-analytic findings (see 2 and 3 in overview of part 1).

N.B. the meta-analytic findings are on scaled measures. If you want to compare your results with them, you need to scale your measures as well: subtract the mean, and divide by the standard deviation. N.N.B. We want to think carefully about fixed and random effects in our model. In particular: how should study be included? Does it make sense to have all studies put together? Does it make sense to analyze both languages together? Relatedly: does it make sense to scale all data from all studies together? N.N.N.B. If you want to estimate the studies separately, you can try this syntax: Feature ~ 0 + Study + Study:Diagnosis + [your randomEffects]. Now you’ll have an intercept per each study (the estimates for the controls) and an effect of diagnosis per each study

N.B. Remember to save the acoustic features of voice in a separate file, so to be able to load them next time

Reminder of the report to write

Part 1 - Can we find a difference in acoustic features in schizophrenia?

  1. Describe your sample number of studies, number of participants, age, gender, clinical and cognitive features of the two groups. Furthemore, critically assess whether the groups (schizophrenia and controls) are balanced. N.B. you need to take studies into account.

  2. Describe the acoustic profile of a schizophrenic voice: which features are different? E.g. People with schizophrenia tend to have high-pitched voice, and present bigger swings in their prosody than controls. N.B. look also at effect sizes. How do these findings relate to the meta-analytic findings?

  3. Discuss the analysis necessary to replicate the meta-analytic findings Look at the results reported in the paper (see meta-analysis in the readings) and see whether they are similar to those you get. 3.1) Check whether significance and direction of the effects are similar 3.2) Standardize your outcome, run the model and check whether the beta’s is roughly matched (matched with hedge’s g) which fixed and random effects should be included, given your dataset? E.g. what about language and study, age and gender? Discuss also how studies and languages should play a role in your analyses. E.g. should you analyze each study individually? Or each language individually? Or all together? Each of these choices makes some assumptions about how similar you expect the studies/languages to be.

  • Your report should look like a methods paragraph followed by a result paragraph in a typical article (think the Communication and Cognition paper)
